summary: The primary type of chemical bonding involved when a glass ionomer cement interacts with dental enamel or dentin is an ionic bond due to its specific composition involving carboxylic acids reacting with metal cations from hydroxyapatite crystals present naturally within teeth tissues for strong adhesion properties necessary during restorative procedures like fillings where long-term fluoride release also occurs beneficially against decay progression over time as mentioned across multiple references reviewed here today regarding this topic area specifically focusing upon understanding what kind of molecular interactions occur at interfaces formed after application of such materials onto prepared surfaces prior to curing process initiation leading up towards final setting stage completion once all reactive components have fully reacted together resulting into formation cohesive network capable providing mechanical strength needed while simultaneously releasing therapeutic agents continuously throughout service life span expected based off initial dosage applied initially immediately post-application phase concluding our analysis now moving forward toward
